name: Refugee Medical Assistance (RMA)
organization: Office of Refugee Resettlement (ORR), US Department of HHS
description: Refugee Medical Assistance (RMA) provides up to 12 months of healthcare coverage to newly arrived refugees, asylees, and other eligible humanitarian immigrants who are not eligible for Medicaid or CHIP. RMA covers essential medical services including doctor visits, prescriptions, hospitalization, mental health services, and preventive care. The program is administered through state refugee coordinators and is designed to bridge the healthcare gap during the initial resettlement period before individuals can access employer-sponsored insurance or other long-term coverage.
eligibility: Refugees, asylees, Cuban/Haitian entrants, victims of trafficking, and Special Immigrant Visa holders who have been in the US for less than 12 months and are not eligible for Medicaid or CHIP.
applicationProcess: Apply through your state refugee coordinator or local resettlement agency. Eligibility is assessed automatically during the refugee resettlement intake process.
geographicScope: United States - Nationwide (administered by state refugee coordinators)
documentsRequired: Proof of refugee/asylee status, I-94 arrival record, date of entry documentation, Social Security Number (if available)

name: HRSA Health Center Program — Free & Sliding-Scale Care
organization: Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA), US Department of HHS
description: HRSA-funded Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs) provide comprehensive primary healthcare services to anyone regardless of immigration status, insurance coverage, or ability to pay. Over 1,400 health centers operate across 15,000+ sites nationwide, serving nearly 32 million patients annually. Services include primary care, dental care, mental health and substance use disorder treatment, vision care, and pharmacy services. Fees are based on a sliding scale tied to income, and no one is turned away for inability to pay. These centers are a critical safety net for undocumented immigrants and those without insurance.
eligibility: Open to all individuals regardless of immigration status, insurance, or ability to pay. Sliding-scale fees based on household income.
amount: Free or sliding-scale fees based on income
applicationProcess: Visit findahealthcenter.hrsa.gov to locate a nearby health center. Walk-ins are accepted at most locations, or call to schedule an appointment.
documentsRequired: Proof of income for sliding-scale fee determination. No immigration documents required.
targetDiagnosis: General - primary care, dental, mental health, vision, pharmacy

name: Canada Interim Federal Health Program (IFHP)
description: The Interim Federal Health Program (IFHP) provides limited, temporary healthcare coverage to protected persons, resettled refugees, and refugee claimants in Canada who are not yet eligible for provincial or territorial health insurance. IFHP covers basic healthcare services including hospital and physician services, laboratory and diagnostic tests, prescription medications, prenatal and obstetrical care, immunizations, and urgent dental and vision care. Supplemental coverage includes mental health counseling, assistive devices, and home care services for those with demonstrated need.
eligibility: Resettled refugees, protected persons, refugee claimants, and certain other groups designated by the Minister of Immigration. Coverage continues until eligible for provincial/territorial health insurance.
amount: Full coverage for basic healthcare services; supplemental coverage for additional needs
applicationProcess: IFHP coverage is provided automatically to eligible individuals upon receiving refugee status or submitting a refugee claim. Present your IFHP certificate to healthcare providers.
documentsRequired: IFHP certificate of eligibility (issued by IRCC), refugee claim or status documents, government-issued photo ID

name: USCRI Refugee Health Services
organization: US Committee for Refugees and Immigrants (USCRI)
description: USCRI's Refugee Health Services program provides comprehensive health navigation and direct health services to newly arrived refugees and immigrants across multiple US locations. Services include initial health screenings, mental health assessments, connection to primary care providers, health education and literacy programs, chronic disease management support, and assistance enrolling in Medicaid, CHIP, or marketplace insurance. USCRI's trained community health workers bridge language and cultural barriers to ensure immigrants can access appropriate healthcare in their communities.
eligibility: Refugees, asylees, and immigrants in USCRI service areas seeking healthcare navigation and support.
applicationProcess: Contact USCRI through their website or visit a local USCRI office to request health services. No referral needed.
geographicScope: United States - Multiple offices nationwide (Virginia, Texas, New York, Vermont, and others)
documentsRequired: Proof of refugee or immigrant status, any existing medical records
targetDiagnosis: General - health screenings, mental health, chronic disease management

name: Emergency Medicaid for Non-Citizens
description: Emergency Medicaid provides coverage for emergency medical conditions to individuals who would otherwise qualify for Medicaid but are ineligible due to immigration status, including undocumented immigrants. The program covers treatment of emergency medical conditions including labor and delivery, acute care, emergency surgery, and treatment of conditions that could result in serious harm if not immediately addressed. Coverage is available in all 50 states and is federally mandated. This is often the only publicly funded healthcare option for undocumented immigrants facing medical emergencies.
eligibility: Individuals who meet Medicaid income requirements but are ineligible for full Medicaid due to immigration status, including undocumented immigrants. Must have an emergency medical condition.
amount: Full coverage for emergency medical treatment
applicationProcess: Apply through your state Medicaid agency or at the hospital during an emergency. Hospitals are required to screen and stabilize patients regardless of insurance or immigration status under EMTALA.
geographicScope: United States - All 50 states and territories
documentsRequired: Proof of income, proof of residency in the state, medical documentation of emergency condition. Immigration documents are NOT required.
targetDiagnosis: Emergency medical conditions - labor/delivery, acute care, emergency surgery

name: Cures Within Reach ReGRoW Program
description: The Cures Within Reach ReGRoW (Research Grants Worldwide) program funds clinical repurposing trials led by researchers in low and lower-middle income countries. The program provides grants of up to $70,000 for clinical trials that test existing, approved drugs for new disease indications. This is particularly relevant for patients in developing countries who lack access to expensive new therapies, as drug repurposing can bring treatments to market faster and at lower cost. The program supports researchers from any eligible country and focuses on diseases with high unmet medical need.
eligibility: Researchers based in low or lower-middle income countries (as classified by the World Bank). Must propose a clinical repurposing trial for an existing approved drug.
applicationProcess: Submit a research proposal through the Cures Within Reach website during open application cycles. Proposals are peer-reviewed by scientific advisory committees.
geographicScope: Low and lower-middle income countries worldwide
documentsRequired: Research proposal, institutional review board approval, investigator CV, budget justification, letter of institutional support
targetDiagnosis: Any disease with high unmet need — drug repurposing focus

name: ERDERA 2026 Joint Transnational Call — Rare Disease Research
description: The ERDERA 2026 Joint Transnational Call supports international collaborative research projects focused on rare diseases. This call brings together funding organizations from EU member states, associated countries, and Canada to co-fund cutting-edge research. Projects must involve researchers from at least three participating countries. Research areas include understanding disease mechanisms, developing diagnostics, and advancing therapeutic approaches for rare diseases. The program is particularly relevant for immigrant patients with rare diseases who may benefit from cross-border research collaboration and access to specialized expertise unavailable in their home countries.
eligibility: Research teams from at least 3 participating countries (EU member states, associated countries, Canada). Principal investigators must be affiliated with eligible research institutions.
amount: Varies by national funding organization (typically EUR 100,000-500,000 per consortium)
applicationProcess: Submit a pre-proposal during the open call period via the ERDERA submission portal. Shortlisted teams are invited to submit full proposals. Each national team must also apply to their national funding organization.
geographicScope: European Union, associated countries, and Canada
documentsRequired: Research proposal, consortium agreement, ethics approval, institutional endorsement, budget per partner
